help.overview: The Stereographic Map projects the selected satellite's ground trace onto a flat map of Earth. It shows where the satellite is right now and the path it will follow over the coming orbit. When a sensor is selected, it also shows exactly when the satellite will be visible to that sensor. The map updates automatically as simulation time advances.
help.imgAlt: Stereographic map showing a satellite ground trace with in-view segments highlighted
help.imgCaption: A satellite ground trace. The satellite icon marks the current position; radar icons mark selected sensors.
help.readingTheMap:
• Satellite icon: the satellite's current position. It moves as time advances.
  • Yellow trace and dots: parts of the orbit where the satellite is inside a selected sensor's field of view.
  • Red trace and dots: parts of the orbit where no selected sensor can see the satellite. With no sensor selected, the whole trace is red.
  • Blue trace: the ground trace of the secondary selected satellite over the same time window. Segments visible to a selected sensor turn cyan.
  • Radar icons: the ground locations of the currently selected sensors.
help.howToUse:
1. Select a satellite, for example by searching for "ISS" and clicking a result.
  2. Optionally select a sensor from the Sensors menu so visibility windows appear in yellow.
  3. Open the Stereo Map from the bottom menu (the icon is disabled until a satellite is selected).
  4. Click any dot along the ground trace to jump the simulation time to the moment the satellite reaches that point.
  5. Use the download button to save the current map as a PNG image.
help.tip1: Click a yellow dot to jump straight into an upcoming sensor pass, then watch the pass unfold on the 3D globe.
help.tip2: The map redraws itself about every 30 seconds; selecting a new satellite or clicking a dot refreshes it immediately.
help.tip3: The map is also available from the command palette: "Toggle Stereo Map" and "Export Stereo Map".
